title: Ask Me Anything: A simple strategy for prompting language models

structured.summary: The paper proposes AMA, a prompting approach that generates multiple open-ended question-answer style prompts via LLM prompt-chains and aggregates their noisy outputs using weak supervision to improve zero- and few-shot task performance. AMA substantially improves robustness and accuracy across diverse NLP benchmarks and models, enabling smaller open-source LLMs to match or surpass few-shot GPT-3 175B without additional training.

strengths.point: Effective use of open-ended QA prompt format
strengths.evidence: Empirically shown that open-ended QA prompts outperform restrictive prompt styles by up to +72% on some tasks, aligning better with LLM pretraining data.

strengths.point: Scalable generation of diverse prompts via prompt-chains
strengths.evidence: Recursive application of LLM functional prompts creates multiple diverse prompt variations automatically, avoiding manual design.

strengths.point: Principled aggregation of noisy prompt outputs using weak supervision
strengths.evidence: Weak supervision models prompt accuracies and dependencies without labeled data, outperforming majority voting by up to 8.7 points.

strengths.point: Strong empirical performance across 20 benchmarks and 14 models
strengths.evidence: AMA yields an average absolute improvement of 10.2% over standard few-shot prompting and enables GPT-J-6B to match/exceed GPT-3 175B on 15/20 tasks.

weaknesses.point: Lack of comparison to chain-of-thought prompting methods
weaknesses.evidence: No extensive experiments or citations contrasting AMA with CoT prompting, which also promote free-form generation and reasoning.

weaknesses.point: Limited ablations on prompt-chain quantity and impact of prompt format vs. aggregation
weaknesses.evidence: No justification or analysis on why 3-6 prompt-chains are used or the separate contributions of prompt format and WS aggregation.

weaknesses.point: Potential unfairness in baselines due to differing computational budgets
weaknesses.evidence: AMA aggregates multiple prompt-chains per input while GPT-3 baselines mostly use single prompts, possibly biasing comparisons.
Ambiguous descriptions of answer-to-label mapping step, prompt-chain generation parameters, terminology inconsistencies, and missing WS failure mode discussion.

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation metrics and generalizability assessment
weaknesses.evidence: Focuses mainly on accuracy; lacks robustness, calibration analyses, or multilingual/multimodal task evaluations.

weaknesses.point: No discussion of computational cost and latency implications
weaknesses.evidence: Running multiple prompt-chains and WS aggregation likely increases inference overhead, which is not analyzed.
Prior work on prompt ensembles and automatic prompt tuning not sufficiently cited or contrasted.

questions.question: How does AMA compare to chain-of-thought prompting in terms of performance and prompt diversity?
questions.motivation: CoT is a prominent recent prompting strategy that promotes free-form reasoning, so understanding AMA's relative benefits is important.

questions.question: What are the computational trade-offs and latency costs of running multiple prompt-chains with WS aggregation?
questions.motivation: For practical deployment, inference speed and resource consumption are critical factors.

questions.question: How robust is AMA to tasks requiring closed-book knowledge or commonsense reasoning?
questions.motivation: Smaller gains on such tasks were noted but not explored in depth, limiting understanding of generality.

questions.question: Can AMA generalize beyond English NLP tasks to multilingual or multimodal domains?
questions.motivation: Broader applicability would increase the method's impact.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of comparison to chain-of-thought prompting methods. No extensive experiments or citations contrasting AMA with CoT prompting, which also promote free-form generation and reasoning
critique_points.point_002: Limited ablations on prompt-chain quantity and impact of prompt format vs. aggregation. No justification or analysis on why 3-6 prompt-chains are used or the separate contributions of prompt format and WS aggregation
critique_points.point_003: Potential unfairness in baselines due to differing computational budgets. AMA aggregates multiple prompt-chains per input while GPT-3 baselines mostly use single prompts, possibly biasing comparisons
critique_points.point_004: Insufficient methodological details and clarity. Ambiguous descriptions of answer-to-label mapping step, prompt-chain generation parameters, terminology inconsistencies, and missing WS failure mode discussion
critique_points.point_005: Limited evaluation metrics and generalizability assessment. Focuses mainly on accuracy; lacks robustness, calibration analyses, or multilingual/multimodal task evaluations
critique_points.point_006: No discussion of computational cost and latency implications. Running multiple prompt-chains and WS aggregation likely increases inference overhead, which is not analyzed
critique_points.point_007: Incomplete related work positioning. Prior work on prompt ensembles and automatic prompt tuning not sufficiently cited or contrasted
